answered.question: How should the parent_scope sentinel(s) work?
answered.answer: Reuse the existing UNSET_SCOPE / ALL_SCOPES pair for both scope and parent_scope. Kwarg name (scope: vs parent_scope:) disambiguates. Thread-local stack stores tuples; sentinel applies to whichever slot is being set.

answered.question: How should the resolver protocol shape look?
answered.answer: Tuple-returning resolver: Config.scope_resolver returns [scope, parent_scope]. Hard breaking change — every consumer with a custom scope_resolver must update on upgrade. Major version bump, explicit CHANGELOG entry, migration note in README. Carves out an exception to the milestone-level BC commitment for resolver shape only.

answered.question: How should Section and Field share the partition-tuple logic?
answered.answer: Inline duplicate the symmetric pattern in field/base.rb and section.rb (mirrors the existing for_entity pattern). Do NOT extract a Scopable concern in this phase. Defer extraction until a third caller appears or implementations diverge.

answered.question: How should `unscoped { }` interact with both keys?
answered.answer: Atomic bypass — unscoped { } drops both scope AND parent_scope predicates entirely. Multimap stays at the field_id level; no scope or parent_scope filtering inside unscoped. Plan-canonical. Preserves today's audit-query semantic.

inferred.text: Resolver protocol shape change is a documented breaking change for the v0.2 release; CHANGELOG and README must include migration notes for custom Config.scope_resolver implementations.
spec/regressions/review_round_2_scope_leak_spec.rb and review_round_3_collision_spec.rb gain parallel coverage for the parent_scope axis (atomic-bypass semantic preserved).

deferred.idea: Extract a shared Scopable concern between Section and Field once a third caller appears or implementations diverge.

Auto-coerce non-array returns from custom resolvers as [value, nil] (BC-shim alternative). Explicitly rejected in favor of the hard-break path; documented to prevent future reintroduction without a deliberate reversal.
